title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
Module 4: Moving to GitHub
Your local repository is powerful, but it’s only on your machine. GitHub brings three things:

Backup — your history lives online
Collaboration — multiple people can contribute
Public sharing — others can use your code

First: connect securely. That’s SSH.

title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
SSH Authentication — setup
SSH lets your machine authenticate to GitHub without passwords:
# Check for existing key
ls -al ~/.ssh

# Generate new key (if needed)
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your.email@uzh.ch"

# Copy public key → paste into github.com/settings/keys
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub

# Test connection
ssh -T git@github.com






Note


The private key never leaves your machine. GitHub only ever sees the public key — that’s why it’s safe to share the public key, and the private key is not.

title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
SSH troubleshooting



Error
Cause
Solution




Permission denied
Private key not found
Run ssh-keygen


Connection refused
Firewall blocking
Use HTTPS instead


Agent refused operation
SSH agent not running
eval "$(ssh-agent -s)"


Public key not on GitHub
Key not uploaded
Paste in settings/keys

title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
SSH troubleshooting — fallback
If SSH still doesn’t work, use HTTPS instead:
git clone https://github.com/USERNAME/REPO.git
git remote add origin https://github.com/USERNAME/REPO.git
HTTPS is simpler for first-time users and doesn’t require SSH key setup.

title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
Connecting local to remote
# Link your local repo to GitHub
git remote add origin git@github.com:USERNAME/REPO.git

# Verify
git remote -v
origin is just the conventional name for your primary remote.

title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
git push
Share your commits with GitHub:
# First time — push and set upstream
git push -u origin main

# Every time after
git push







Note


git push only uploads commits. Uncommitted changes stay local.

title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
git pull
Get the latest changes from GitHub:
git pull
Under the hood this is two steps:
git fetch    # download new commits
git merge    # merge them into your branch

Run git pull at the start of every work session.

title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
git clone
Copy an entire repository to your machine:
git clone git@github.com:DQBM-CCCZ/intro_git_github.git

# Into a custom folder name
git clone git@github.com:DQBM-CCCZ/intro_git_github.git my-folder
Clone includes the full history and sets up origin automatically.

title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
The full remote workflow
Every command you’ll actually use them, in order:
git clone <url>           # 1. get the repo (first time only)
git checkout -b feature   # 2. work on a branch
# make changes...
git add --all             # 3. stage
git commit -m "message"   # 4. commit
git push origin feature   # 5. push to GitHub
# 6. open Pull Request on GitHub
# 7. after merge: git checkout main && git pull
This is the complete workflow for collaborating on GitHub.

title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
Pull Request workflow (Part 1)
Setup and branch:





graph LR
    A["Your Fork<br/>on GitHub"] -->|git clone| B["Local Machine"]
    B -->|git checkout<br/>-b feature| C["Feature Branch"]
    C -->|edit files<br/>git add<br/>git commit| D["Commits ready"]
    D -->|git push<br/>origin feature| E["Push to Fork"]
    
    style A fill:#e3f2fd
    style B fill:#f3e5f5
    style C fill:#e8f5e9
    style E fill:#fff3e0

title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
Pull Request workflow (Part 2)
Review and merge:





graph LR
    A["Pull Request<br/>on Main Repo"] -->|Code Review| B{"Approved?"}
    B -->|Yes| C["Merge to main"]
    B -->|No| D["Revise & push<br/>to feature"]
    D -->|Updates show<br/>in PR| A
    C -->|git checkout main<br/>git pull| E["Local<br/>up-to-date"]
    
    style A fill:#fff3e0
    style C fill:#c8e6c9
    style E fill:#bbdefb

title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
GitHub Issues workflow (Part 1)
Planning and setup:





graph TD
    A["🆕 Create Issue"] --> B["👤 Assign &<br/>Add labels"]
    B --> C["🏗️ In Progress"]
    C --> D["💻 Create branch<br/>git checkout -b fix-X"]
    
    style A fill:#ffebee
    style B fill:#fff9c4
    style C fill:#fff3e0
    style D fill:#e1f5fe

title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
GitHub Issues workflow (Part 2)
Code and closure:





graph TD
    A["✅ Commit work<br/>closes #issue-number"] --> B["🔀 Create PR"]
    B --> C["✔️ Review<br/>& Merge"]
    C --> D["🎯 Issue<br/>Auto-closes"]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5fe
    style D fill:#c8e6c9

title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
Merge conflict resolution (Part 1)
Detect and inspect:





graph TD
    A["⚠️ Two branches<br/>changed same lines"] --> B["❌ git merge fails"]
    B --> C["📖 Open conflicted<br/>file"]
    C --> D["See conflict markers:<br/><<<<<<< HEAD<br/>your changes<br/>=======<br/>their changes<br/>>>>>>>>"]
    
    style A fill:#ffcdd2
    style B fill:#ffcdd2
    style D fill:#fff9c4

title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
Merge conflict resolution (Part 2)
Decide and complete:





graph TD
    A["Choose: Keep<br/>yours/theirs/both?"] --> B["Remove markers"]
    B --> C["git add &<br/>git commit"]
    C --> D["✅ Complete"]
    
    style A fill:#fff9c4
    style D fill:#c8e6c9

title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
GitHub Issues
Track tasks, bugs, and discussions — all in one place.

Bug report: “Script crashes on paired-end input”
Feature request: “Add support for BAM files”
Task: “Write documentation for pipeline module”


Reference issues in commit messages:
git commit -m "fixed alignment bug, closes #12"
→ Automatically closes issue #12 when the PR is merged.

title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
GitHub Projects
Issues are individual cards. A Project is the board that organizes them.
| To Do      | In Progress | Done       |
|------------|-------------|------------|
| Issue #1   | Issue #3    | Issue #2   |
| Issue #4   |             |            |
Drag cards as work progresses. Link issues so they move automatically when a PR closes them.

title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
Pull Requests
We planned the work with Issues and Projects. Now the actual code change: a Pull Request is a proposal to merge your branch into another branch.





flowchart LR
    A[your-branch] -->|Pull Request| B[main]
    B -->|review + discuss| A
    A -->|merge| B






PR workflow: 1. Push your branch: git push origin my-feature 2. Open PR on GitHub: Compare & pull request 3. Write a description: what changed, why, issue reference 4. Request a reviewer 5. Address review comments with new commits 6. Merge

title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
Fork vs Clone









Fork
Clone




Where
GitHub (server copy)
Your machine (local copy)


When
Contributing to someone else’s repo
Working on any repo locally


Push to
Your fork → PR to original
Original (if you have permission)



Fork workflow:





flowchart LR
    A[Fork on GitHub] --> B[Clone your fork]
    B --> C[Branch]
    C --> D[Edit & commit]
    D --> E[Push to your fork]
    E --> F[Pull Request to original]

title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
Exercise 5 — Push & Pull
See Exercise 5

Create GitHub repo (no README)
git remote add origin <ssh-url>
git push -u origin main
Edit README on GitHub → git pull locally
Edit locally → git push → check on GitHub

title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
Exercise 6 — Fork & Pull Request
See Exercise 6

Fork DQBM-CCCZ/intro_git_github
Clone your fork
git checkout -b YOUR_FIRST_NAME
Add your section to projects.md
git push origin YOUR_FIRST_NAME
Open a Pull Request → reference your issue

title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
You’re done! 🎉
You’ve learned:

Why version control matters (Module 1)
How to use Git locally (Module 2)
How to work safely with branches (Module 3)
How to collaborate on GitHub (Module 4)

title: Module 4: GitHub Collaboration
text:
Your new skills ✅
✅ Version-control your analysis and code
✅ Try new approaches without breaking what works
✅ Collaborate with colleagues
✅ Contribute to open-source projects
Next: These skills apply to everything. Take them forward.

title: Module 2: Local Git
text:
Module 2: Let’s build something
You know why Git matters. Now time to actually use it.
We’ll build a realistic project — experiment-tracker — tracking one analysis from start to finish. Every Git concept you learn applies immediately.

title: Module 2: Local Git
text:
Getting started
First, check Git is installed and tell it who you are:
git --version

git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your.email@uzh.ch"
These are embedded in every commit you make.

title: Module 2: Local Git
text:
There are many git commands…
Before diving in, a reality check — Git is a big tool:
add       clone     commit    diff      fetch     init
log       merge     pull      push      remote    reset
branch    checkout  stash     status    tag       ...

We will focus on about 12 of them today.
The rest you can look up when you need them — git help <command>

title: Module 2: Local Git
text:
Common Git gotchas
Before you dive in, three things that surprise beginners:







Warning


Git tracks files, not folders - Empty folders don’t show up in Git - Workaround: put a .gitkeep file in empty folders you need
Never delete the .git folder - It contains your entire history - If you accidentally delete it, all commits are lost - Backup important repos to GitHub as a safety net
.gitignore doesn’t un-track files - If a file was already committed, adding it to .gitignore won’t remove it - First remove it: git rm --cached filename - Then add to .gitignore, then commit











Tip


When in doubt, git status and git log are your friends. They never lie.

title: Module 2: Local Git
text:
What you’ll practice today
Next, you’ll build this workflow hands-on:

git init — create a repository
git add → git commit — save snapshots
git diff — inspect changes
git log — explore history

Ready? Let’s go.

title: Module 2: Local Git
section: A running example: tracking an experiment
text:
A running example: tracking an experiment
Let’s make those 12 commands concrete. Throughout today we will build one realistic project — experiment-tracker — the kind of folder you might keep next to a real experiment: sample metadata, analysis notes, and a results summary. No programming language required.
cd ~/Downloads
mkdir experiment-tracker
cd experiment-tracker
git init
ls -a    # confirm the hidden .git folder was created
The .git folder is where Git stores everything. Never delete it.

title: Module 2: Local Git
text:
Tracking changes — the three stages
Every change you make moves through three places, in order:
Working Directory → (git add) → Staging Area → (git commit) → Repository

Why a staging area? Imagine you fixed a bug and started an unrelated experiment in the same session. The staging area lets you commit the bug fix alone, with its own clear message — without dragging in the half-finished experiment.

title: Module 2: Local Git
text:
The staging area: explained

Three places where your code lives:

Working Directory — your files, as you’re editing them
Staging Area — you choose what goes into the next commit
Repository — your permanent history, safe and backed up

title: Module 2: Local Git
text:
How staging works
You edit file.txt
        ↓ (git add file.txt)
File is now STAGED (ready to commit)
        ↓ (git commit -m "message")
File is now in REPOSITORY (permanent history)
Key insight: Between editing and committing, you can choose exactly what goes into each snapshot. This is the power of staging.

title: Module 2: Local Git
text:
The Git workflow: visual recap
Beginners ask: “How often should I commit?”

One commit = one logical unit of work
Good commits: - “added sample metadata for 4 samples” - “fixed normalization bug in analysis script” - “documented methods section” - “refactored QC function for clarity”


Avoid: - “stuff I worked on today” (too vague) - “fixed bug and added feature and updated docs” (too many things)








Tip


Rule of thumb: If your commit message needs the word “and” in it, split it into two commits.

title: Module 2: Local Git
text:
Your first file: sample metadata
Your repository exists but is empty. Let’s put something in the working directory — step 1 of the three stages:
nano samples.csv
sample_id,condition,replicate
S01,control,1
S02,control,2
S03,treated,1
S04,treated,2
Ctrl+X → Y → Enter to save and close.

title: Module 2: Local Git
text:
git status
The file exists, but Git hasn’t been told to track it. Check what Git sees:
git status
On branch main

No commits yet

Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
        samples.csv
Git sees the file but is not tracking it yet.

title: Module 2: Local Git
text:
git add → git commit

Terminal


# Stage the file
git add samples.csv

git status   # now it shows "Changes to be committed"

# Commit
git commit -m "added sample metadata for 4 samples"

git status   # now: "nothing to commit, working tree clean"






Tip


The CLI does the same thing underneath. Use the terminal when you’re comfortable, or any tool that feels natural.

title: Module 2: Local Git
text:
git log
git log
commit ac3eveta1234...
Author: Deepak Tanwar <deepak@uzh.ch>
Date:   Mon Jun 23 09:45:00 2025

    added sample metadata for 4 samples
git log --oneline   # compact view

title: Module 2: Local Git
text:
Writing good commit messages
Your commit message is a note to future-you (or a labmate) explaining why you made this change.
Good format:
imperative verb: what changed

Optional longer explanation here if needed.
Can reference an issue: "closes #42"
Examples: - add qc step to analysis pipeline - fix alignment scoring bug in normalization - update readme with installation instructions

title: Module 2: Local Git
text:
Commit message anti-patterns
Avoid vague or multi-purpose messages:
❌ Bad examples: - "fixed stuff" - "work in progress" - "as requested" - "I changed the code again"

title: Module 2: Local Git
section: Pro tip: Reference issues in commits
text:
Pro tip: Reference issues in commits
Link commits to GitHub issues automatically:
git commit -m "fix duplicate sample handling, closes #7"
This automatically closes issue #7 when you merge to main.

title: Module 2: Local Git
text:
The staging area recap

The staging area is your safety net: you can see exactly what will go into your next commit before it’s permanent.

title: Module 2: Local Git
text:
HEAD
HEAD = pointer to the latest commit on your current branch.

When you make a new commit, HEAD moves forward automatically.






Tip


Think of HEAD as a sticky note that says “you are here” — it always points to the tip of whichever branch you’re currently on.

title: Module 2: Local Git
text:
git diff
Diffing shows what changed between two states:


Terminal


# Working directory vs staging area (unstaged changes)
git diff

# Staging area vs last commit (what will be in next commit)
git diff --staged

# Working directory vs last commit (everything changed)
git diff HEAD

# Two specific commits
git diff <hash1> <hash2>
Terminal diffs use +/- prefixes per line — green + for added, red - for removed.

title: Module 2: Local Git
text:
Exercise 3 — Local Git Workflow
You just learned the gotchas, the one-unit rule, and commit message best practices.
See Exercise 3 for the full walkthrough.
Part 1: Setup & tracking

mkdir experiment-tracker && cd experiment-tracker && git init
Create samples.csv with sample metadata, git add, git commit
Create analysis_log.md, document steps with clear commit messages

title: Module 2: Local Git
text:
Exercise 3 continued
Part 2: Inspecting history

git log --oneline — see all your commits
git diff before staging; git diff --staged after staging
Compare commits: git diff HASH1 HASH2

Key practice: Write commit messages using the “one-unit rule” and imperative format.

title: Module 2: Local Git
text:
Ready for Module 3?
You’ve mastered the local workflow: init → commit → log → diff.
Your repository is on your machine only. Next: create branches to try new ideas safely, merge results back, and handle conflicts.
You now have the power to experiment without fear. 🌳

title: Markdown Cheat Sheet
text: Every Markdown feature you’re likely to need on GitHub, with the raw syntax on the left and how it actually renders on the right.

text:
Headings


Raw

## Heading 2
### Heading 3
#### Heading 4

Rendered

Heading 2

Heading 3

Heading 4

text:
Heading 2

Heading 3

Heading 4

text:
Emphasis


Raw
**bold text**
*italic text*
***bold and italic***
~~strikethrough~~

Rendered
bold text
italic text
bold and italic
strikethrough

title: Markdown Cheat Sheet
text:
Blockquotes


Raw
> This is a quote.
>
> It can span multiple lines.

Rendered

This is a quote.
It can span multiple lines.

- Third item

Links


Raw

https://github.com

Reference-style links


title: Markdown Cheat Sheet
text:
Mentions and issue references (GitHub only)
These only become clickable links when viewed on GitHub.com — they render as plain text everywhere else (including this page).
@dktanwar please review

See issue #12 for details






Note



On GitHub, @username notifies that person, and #12 automatically links to issue or PR number 12 in the same repository.

title: Final Exercise
text:
End of day | ~45 minutes
This exercise ties together everything from the course into one complete workflow.

title: Final Exercise
text:
Part 1 — Plan on GitHub (~15 min)

1a. Create an Issue

Go to https://github.com/DQBM-CCCZ/intro_git_github
Click Issues → New issue
Title: YOUR_NAME — project description
Body: list 3–5 things you plan to add
Assign to yourself, add a label (e.g., documentation)
Submit — note your issue number (e.g., #22)



1b. Add to the Project board

On the issue page, find Projects in the right sidebar
Add to the course project board → move it to In Progress

title: Final Exercise
text:
Part 2 — Work locally (~15 min)

2a. Clone the course repo
cd ~/Downloads
git clone git@github.com:DQBM-CCCZ/intro_git_github.git course_repo
cd course_repo
git log --oneline


2b. Create a branch
git checkout -b YOUR_FIRST_NAME


2c. Add your project description
nano projects.md
# Your Full Name
Your project description here.


2d. Commit with your issue number
git add projects.md
git commit -m "added YOUR_NAME project description, closes #YOUR_ISSUE_NUMBER"

title: Final Exercise
section: Part 3 — Push and Pull Request (~10 min)
text:
Part 3 — Push and Pull Request (~10 min)
git push origin YOUR_FIRST_NAME
On GitHub:

Click Compare & pull request
Base: main ← Compare: YOUR_FIRST_NAME
Title: Added YOUR_NAME project description
Description: Closes #YOUR_ISSUE_NUMBER
Create pull request

title: Final Exercise
section: Part 4 — After all PRs are merged (~5 min)
text:
Part 4 — After all PRs are merged (~5 min)
git checkout main
git pull origin main
git log --oneline    # see everyone's contributions

title: Final Exercise
section: Final challenge — no peeking until you’ve tried
text:
Final challenge — no peeking until you’ve tried
Imagine: it’s three months from now. A collaborator asks “can you re-run exactly the analysis that produced the numbers in Table 2?” You don’t remember which version of your script that was.
Question: Using only what you learned today, how would you find and re-run that exact version?






TipClick to reveal one possible approach






git log --oneline to scan commit messages for something like “generated Table 2” or a date near submission
git diff <hash1> <hash2> to confirm what changed between candidate commits
git checkout <hash> to put your working directory back exactly as it was at that commit (detached HEAD — fine for read-only inspection)
Re-run your script from that exact state
git checkout main to return to the latest version when done

This is the entire reason version control exists for research: not just backup, but exact reproducibility of any past state, on demand.

title: Final Exercise
text:
What you demonstrated today



Skill
Module




Version control concepts
1


Markdown formatting
1


init, add, commit, log, diff
2


Branching, merging, conflict resolution
3


.gitignore, git reset
3


SSH authentication
4


push, pull, clone, remote
4


Issues and Projects
4


Fork and Pull Request
4

title: Final Exercise
text:
Next steps

Use Git for your own scripts from day one
Branch whenever you try something experimental
Write meaningful commit messages — your future self will thank you
Reference: Git Cheat Sheet · Markdown Cheat Sheet

title: Exercise 5 — Push Your Local Repo to GitHub
text: Module 4 | ~25 minutes

title: Exercise 5 — Push Your Local Repo to GitHub
text:
Goal
Connect your local repository to GitHub and push your work online.
You’ve learned how — now practice the SSH setup and push workflow.

title: Exercise 5 — Push Your Local Repo to GitHub
text:
Prerequisite: SSH is working
Test your SSH key:
ssh -T git@github.com
# Expected: Hi YOUR_USERNAME! You've successfully authenticated.
If this fails: See the troubleshooting table from Module 4 slide “If SSH connection fails…” for solutions. Common issues: private key not found, agent not running, or public key not uploaded to GitHub.

title: Exercise 5 — Push Your Local Repo to GitHub
section: Part A — Create the GitHub repository
text:
Part A — Create the GitHub repository

Go to https://github.com/new
Name it experiment-tracker (same as your local folder)
Set to Public
Do NOT initialize with a README (your local repo already has files)
Click Create repository

Copy the SSH URL shown on the next page.

title: Exercise 5 — Push Your Local Repo to GitHub
text:
Part B — Connect and push
cd ~/Downloads/experiment-tracker

# Add GitHub as the remote
git remote add origin git@github.com:YOUR_USERNAME/experiment-tracker.git

# Verify
git remote -v

# Push (and set upstream)
git push -u origin main

title: Exercise 5 — Push Your Local Repo to GitHub
text:
Part C — Verify on GitHub

Go to https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/experiment-tracker
samples.csv, analysis_log.md, results_summary.md and their commit history should be there
Click commits to explore the history
Click a commit hash to see exactly what changed

title: Exercise 5 — Push Your Local Repo to GitHub
section: Part D — Edit on GitHub, pull locally
text:
Part D — Edit on GitHub, pull locally

On GitHub, click results_summary.md → pencil icon
Add a line: Reviewed by collaborator on GitHub.
Commit: added review note on GitHub

Now pull that change:
git pull
cat results_summary.md    # new line is here

title: Exercise 5 — Push Your Local Repo to GitHub
text:
Check your understanding
Question: What would have happened if you had a local, uncommitted edit to results_summary.md when you ran git pull just now?






TipClick to reveal the answer





git pull would likely refuse, with a message like error: Your local changes to the following files would be overwritten by merge. Git protects you from silently losing uncommitted work. You’d need to either commit your changes first, or temporarily set them aside with git stash, pull, then git stash pop to bring them back.



Question: You used git push -u origin main the first time, but just git push every time after. Why does the first push need the extra flag?






TipClick to reveal the answer





-u (short for --set-upstream) tells Git “remember that my local main branch tracks origin/main.” Once that link is recorded, plain git push and git pull know where to send/fetch without you specifying the remote and branch every time.

title: Exercise 5 — Push Your Local Repo to GitHub
text:
Part E — Full cycle
nano analysis_log.md      # add a "Step 4 — Visualization" note locally
git add analysis_log.md
git commit -m "added visualization step"
git push

# Check GitHub — your change appears immediately

title: Exercise 5 — Push Your Local Repo to GitHub
text:
What you learned



Command
What it does




git remote add origin <url>
Connect to GitHub


git remote -v
Show connections


git push -u origin main
Push and set upstream


git push
Push new commits


git pull
Fetch + merge from GitHub

title: Exercise 3 — Local Git Workflow
text: Module 2 | ~35 minutes

title: Exercise 3 — Local Git Workflow
text:
Goal
Build a small, realistic project — experiment-tracker — and practice the core local Git workflow: init → add → commit → log → diff.
This is the running example we will keep extending for the rest of the day.
As you work: Practice the one-unit rule for commits and use clear, imperative commit messages from the slides (e.g., “add QC step” not “stuff done”).

title: Exercise 3 — Local Git Workflow
text:
Part A — Create the repository
cd ~/Downloads

mkdir experiment-tracker
cd experiment-tracker

git init
ls -a     # confirm .git was created

title: Exercise 3 — Local Git Workflow
section: Part B — First commit: sample metadata
text:
Part B — First commit: sample metadata
nano samples.csv
sample_id,condition,replicate
S01,control,1
S02,control,2
S03,treated,1
S04,treated,2
Save and close: Ctrl+X → Y → Enter
git status              # git sees the file but isn't tracking it
git add samples.csv
git status              # now it's staged
git commit -m "added sample metadata for 4 samples"
git log






TipClick to see expected output of git status (before staging)





On branch main

No commits yet

Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
        samples.csv

nothing added to commit but untracked files present (use "git add" to track)



Question: After git add samples.csv, you run git status again. Before checking the answer below — what do you think the wording changes to?






TipClick to reveal the answer





On branch main

No commits yet

Changes to be committed:
  (use "git rm --cached <file>..." to unstage)
        new file:   samples.csv
“Untracked” became “Changes to be committed” — the file moved from the working directory into the staging area.

title: Exercise 3 — Local Git Workflow
section: Part C — More commits: documenting analysis steps
text:
Part C — More commits: documenting analysis steps
nano analysis_log.md
# Analysis Log

## Step 1 — Quality control
Checked all 4 samples for read quality. All passed QC thresholds.
git add analysis_log.md
git commit -m "added analysis log with QC step"

nano analysis_log.md
Append:
## Step 2 — Normalization
Applied log2 normalization to raw counts before comparison.
git add analysis_log.md
git commit -m "documented normalization step"

git log --oneline    # see all 3 commits

title: Exercise 3 — Local Git Workflow
section: Part D — Inspect changes with diff
text:
Part D — Inspect changes with diff
nano analysis_log.md   # add a "Step 3 — Differential expression" section
Before staging:
git diff               # working directory vs staging area
After staging:
git add analysis_log.md
git diff --staged      # staging area vs last commit
git commit -m "documented differential expression step"
Compare your first and last commit:
git log --oneline             # note the two hashes
git diff FIRST_HASH LAST_HASH

title: Exercise 3 — Local Git Workflow
text:
Check your understanding
Question: You ran git diff before staging and saw nothing printed, even though you definitely edited analysis_log.md. What did you forget?






TipClick to reveal the answer





You probably forgot to save the file in nano before running git diff — Git can only see what’s actually written to disk. (Or, less likely, you already ran git add for that change, which would move it from git diff into git diff --staged instead.)



Question: What is the difference between git diff and git diff --staged?






TipClick to reveal the answer






git diff → compares your working directory to the staging area (what you’ve changed but not yet staged).
git diff --staged → compares the staging area to the last commit (what will actually go into your next commit).

If you run git add on a file, it “moves” out of the first diff and into the second.

title: Exercise 3 — Local Git Workflow
section: Gotcha: empty folders won’t show up
text:
Gotcha: empty folders won’t show up
If you create an empty folder and commit, Git will ignore it. This sometimes surprises people. If you need an empty folder (e.g., for output files), create an empty .gitkeep file inside:
mkdir output
touch output/.gitkeep
git add output/.gitkeep
git commit -m "add output directory"
This is a harmless trick — .gitkeep has no special meaning to Git. It’s just a placeholder file.



Command
What it does




git init
Create a repository


git status
See what has changed


git add <file>
Stage a file


git commit -m "msg"
Save a snapshot


git log --oneline
View history


git diff
See unstaged changes


git diff --staged
See staged changes

title: Exercise 1 — GitHub Account & First Repository
text: Module 1 | ~20 minutes

title: Exercise 1 — GitHub Account & First Repository
text:
Goal
Create your GitHub account and your first repository.

title: Exercise 1 — GitHub Account & First Repository
text:
Steps

GitHub Account

Go to https://github.com and sign up for a free account
Choose a professional username — you will share this publicly
Verify your email address



Explore GitHub

Go to your profile page: https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME
Find the DQBM-CCCZ organization — check if you are already a member



Create a Repository

Click the + icon (top right) → New repository
Name it exactly your GitHub username (e.g., dktanwar)
Set it to Public
Check Add a README file
Click Create repository



Verify

Go to https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/YOUR_USERNAME
You should see your new repository with a README file
Take a screenshot — we will compare it after Exercise 2

title: Exercise 1 — GitHub Account & First Repository
text:
Check your understanding
Question: You named your repository the exact same as your username. What is special about a repository with this exact name on GitHub?






TipClick to reveal the answer





A repository named exactly like your username is special: GitHub renders its README.md directly on your profile page (github.com/YOUR_USERNAME), not just inside the repository itself. It’s how people build a public “about me” page using nothing but Markdown.



Question: If this repository is Public, who can see it?






TipClick to reveal the answer





Anyone on the internet, including people without a GitHub account. Public repositories are searchable and indexed. A Private repository is only visible to you and people you explicitly invite as collaborators.

title: Exercise 1 — GitHub Account & First Repository
text:
What you learned

How GitHub is organized: users, organizations, repositories
The difference between public and private repositories
A README file is the front page of your repository

title: Exercise 2 — Write Your README with Markdown
text: Module 1 | ~20 minutes

title: Exercise 2 — Write Your README with Markdown
text:
Goal
Edit your GitHub profile README using Markdown formatting.

title: Exercise 2 — Write Your README with Markdown
text:
Steps

Open the README editor

Go to your repository: https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/YOUR_USERNAME
Click the pencil icon on the README.md file to edit it



Write your introduction

Write at least the following sections using Markdown:

# Your Name

## About Me
One or two sentences about your role and research.

## Research Project
Brief description of what you are currently working on.

## Affiliation
- Institution
- Research group / lab

## Education

| Degree | University | Year |
|--------|-----------|------|
| PhD    | UZH       | 2024 |
| MSc    | ...       | ...  |

## Links
- [LinkedIn](https://linkedin.com/in/your-profile)


Commit the file

Scroll down to Commit changes
Write a meaningful commit message: added introduction and education table
Click Commit changes



Compare

Go to https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME
Compare your profile page to the screenshot from Exercise 1 — what changed?

title: Exercise 2 — Write Your README with Markdown
text:
Check your understanding
Question: You wrote # Your Name at the top of the file. What would happen if you used ### instead of #?






TipClick to reveal the answer





It would still render as a heading, but a much smaller one (Heading 3 instead of Heading 1). The number of # symbols sets the heading level — more # = smaller text. This matters for document structure: search engines and screen readers use heading levels to understand a page’s outline, not just its appearance.



Question: Your education table used | to separate columns. What happens if a row has fewer | separators than the header row?






TipClick to reveal the answer





The table breaks — Markdown table renderers expect every row to have the same number of columns as the header. A missing | usually causes the remaining cells in that row to merge into the last column, or the whole table to fail to render as a table at all. Always count your | characters when copy-pasting table rows.

title: Exercise 2 — Write Your README with Markdown
text:
Markdown you practiced



Syntax
Result




# text
Heading 1


## text
Heading 2


**text**
bold


- item
bullet list


| col |
table column


[text](url)
link

title: Exercise 4 — Branching & Merge Conflicts
text: Module 3 | ~40 minutes

title: Exercise 4 — Branching & Merge Conflicts
text:
Goal
Work safely on an experiment branch, merge results back to main, and practice resolving a realistic merge conflict.
You learned why branches are powerful — now experience it hands-on. Continues the experiment-tracker from Exercise 3.

title: Exercise 4 — Branching & Merge Conflicts
section: Part A — Create a branch for a new approach
text:
Part A — Create a branch for a new approach
cd ~/Downloads/experiment-tracker

git branch                              # which branch are you on?
git checkout -b try-new-normalization   # create and switch
git branch                              # confirm

title: Exercise 4 — Branching & Merge Conflicts
section: Part B — Make changes on the branch
text:
Part B — Make changes on the branch
nano analysis_log.md
Append:
## Step 2b — Alternative: quantile normalization
Tried quantile normalization instead of log2. Compare results before merging.
git add analysis_log.md
git commit -m "tried quantile normalization instead of log2"
git log --oneline

title: Exercise 4 — Branching & Merge Conflicts
section: Part C — Merge back to main
text:
Part C — Merge back to main
git checkout main
cat analysis_log.md      # the quantile normalization note is NOT here

git merge try-new-normalization
cat analysis_log.md      # now it IS here

git log --oneline
git branch -d try-new-normalization    # clean up

title: Exercise 4 — Branching & Merge Conflicts
section: Part D — Simulate a merge conflict
text:
Part D — Simulate a merge conflict
git checkout -b draft-conclusion
nano results_summary.md
Write:
# Results Summary
Treated samples show a 2-fold increase in expression compared to control.
git add results_summary.md
git commit -m "drafted conclusion: 2-fold increase"

git checkout main
nano results_summary.md
Write a different conclusion on main:
# Results Summary
Treated samples show no significant change in expression compared to control.
git add results_summary.md
git commit -m "drafted conclusion: no significant change"

git merge draft-conclusion    # CONFLICT
Open the file and find the conflict markers:
<<<<<<< HEAD
Treated samples show no significant change in expression compared to control.
=======
Treated samples show a 2-fold increase in expression compared to control.
>>>>>>> draft-conclusion
Edit to keep the conclusion you actually want (or merge both into one sentence noting the discrepancy needs investigation). Remove all marker lines, then:
git add results_summary.md
git commit -m "resolved conflicting conclusions, flagged for re-analysis"

title: Exercise 4 — Branching & Merge Conflicts
text:
Check your understanding
Question: Why did git merge try-new-normalization in Part C succeed silently, but git merge draft-conclusion in Part D stopped with a conflict?






TipClick to reveal the answer





In Part C, only the try-new-normalization branch had changed analysis_log.md — main hadn’t touched that file since they diverged, so Git could apply the change cleanly (a “fast-forward” or trivial merge).
In Part D, both branches edited the exact same line of results_summary.md after diverging. Git has no way to know which version is “right,” so it pauses the merge and asks you to decide.



Question: While results_summary.md still has conflict markers in it, what does git status say?






TipClick to reveal the answer





On branch main
You have unmerged paths.
  (fix conflicts and run "git commit")

Unmerged paths:
  (use "git add <file>..." to mark resolution)
        both modified:   results_summary.md
Git refuses to let you commit until you resolve and git add the file — this is a safety net so a half-finished conflict can never accidentally become a permanent commit.

title: Exercise 4 — Branching & Merge Conflicts
text:
Part E — .gitignore for raw data
mkdir raw_data
touch raw_data/run1.fastq raw_data/run2.fastq raw_data/instrument.log
git status     # git sees all these files
Create .gitignore:
nano .gitignore
raw_data/
*.log
git status     # ignored files no longer appear
git add .gitignore
git commit -m "add gitignore for raw instrument data"






Warning



Remember from the gotchas: If you had already committed raw_data/run1.fastq, adding it to .gitignore won’t remove it from history. You’d first run:
git rm --cached raw_data/run1.fastq
git commit -m "remove raw data from tracking"
Then add to .gitignore so it won’t happen again.

title: Exercise 4 — Branching & Merge Conflicts
text:
What you learned



Command
What it does




git branch
List branches


git checkout -b <name>
Create and switch


git merge <branch>
Merge into current branch


resolve + git add + git commit
Fix a conflict


.gitignore
Exclude files from tracking









Note



Why this matters for real work: raw sequencing files, large datasets, and instrument logs don’t belong in Git history — they’re huge and usually already backed up elsewhere. .gitignore keeps your repository focused on code, scripts, and small text files that benefit from version control.

title: Exercise 6 — Fork, Branch & Pull Request
text: Module 4 | ~30 minutes

title: Exercise 6 — Fork, Branch & Pull Request
text:
Goal
Contribute to the course repository using the real open-source collaboration workflow: Fork → Clone → Branch → PR.
This is the professional way to contribute to projects you don’t directly own. It’s how GitHub works everywhere.

title: Exercise 6 — Fork, Branch & Pull Request
text:
The Workflow
DQBM-CCCZ/intro_git_github  (original)
        ↓  Fork on GitHub
YOUR_USERNAME/intro_git_github  (your copy)
        ↓  Clone
Your local machine
        ↓  Branch → Edit → Commit → Push
YOUR_USERNAME/intro_git_github  (your copy)
        ↓  Pull Request
DQBM-CCCZ/intro_git_github  (original)

title: Exercise 6 — Fork, Branch & Pull Request
text:
Part A — Fork

Go to https://github.com/DQBM-CCCZ/intro_git_github
Click Fork (top right) → select your account
You now have https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/intro_git_github

title: Exercise 6 — Fork, Branch & Pull Request
text:
Part B — Clone your fork
cd ~/Downloads

git clone git@github.com:YOUR_USERNAME/intro_git_github.git
cd intro_git_github
git log --oneline    # see the existing history

title: Exercise 6 — Fork, Branch & Pull Request
text:
Part C — Branch
git checkout -b YOUR_FIRST_NAME
git branch     # confirm

title: Exercise 6 — Fork, Branch & Pull Request
section: Part D — Add your project description
text:
Part D — Add your project description
nano projects.md
Add a new section:
# Your Full Name
Brief description of your research project.
What question are you answering? What data or tools do you use?
git add projects.md
git status
git commit -m "added YOUR_NAME project description"

title: Exercise 6 — Fork, Branch & Pull Request
section: Part E — Push and open a Pull Request
text:
Part E — Push and open a Pull Request
git push origin YOUR_FIRST_NAME
On GitHub:

Go to https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME/intro_git_github
Click Compare & pull request
Base: DQBM-CCCZ/intro_git_github → main
Title: Added YOUR_NAME project description
Description: Closes #YOUR_ISSUE_NUMBER
Click Create pull request

title: Exercise 6 — Fork, Branch & Pull Request
section: Part F — After all PRs are merged
text:
Part F — After all PRs are merged
# Add the original repo as upstream
git remote add upstream git@github.com:DQBM-CCCZ/intro_git_github.git

# Get everyone's merged contributions
git fetch upstream
git checkout main
git merge upstream/main

git log --oneline    # see all contributions in one place

title: Exercise 6 — Fork, Branch & Pull Request
text:
Check your understanding
Question: You have both origin and upstream remotes configured now. What does each one point to?






TipClick to reveal the answer






origin → your fork (YOUR_USERNAME/intro_git_github) — where you push your own branches.
upstream → the original repo (DQBM-CCCZ/intro_git_github) — where everyone’s pull requests get merged.

This two-remote pattern is the standard way to contribute to a project you don’t have write access to: push to your fork, pull updates from upstream.



Question: Could you have skipped forking and just cloned DQBM-CCCZ/intro_git_github directly?






TipClick to reveal the answer





You could clone it, but you wouldn’t be able to git push your branch to it — you don’t have write permission on someone else’s repository. Forking creates your own writable copy on GitHub first, so git push origin YOUR_BRANCH has somewhere to go. This is exactly how open-source contribution works on every public GitHub project.

title: Exercise 6 — Fork, Branch & Pull Request
text:
What you learned



Term
Meaning




Fork
Server-side copy of a repo under your username


Clone
Local copy of a repo


Upstream
The original repo you forked from


Pull Request
Proposal to merge your branch

title: Introduction to Git & GitHub
text: Welcome to the course website for Introduction to Git and GitHub, including project management.

title: Introduction to Git & GitHub
text:
Acknowledgements







We thank Prof. Michael Krauthammer for supporting this course.
Special thanks to CCCZ — Dr. Maja Zenz and Ms Silke Uflacker — for providing coffee breaks.

title: Introduction to Git & GitHub
text:
Teacher & Author
Deepak Tanwar  Bioinformatics Specialist and Trainer Swiss Institute of Bioinformatics / University of Zurich deepak.tanwar@uzh.ch Bluesky

title: Introduction to Git & GitHub
text:
About This Course
This is a hands-on 1-day workshop for researchers who want to stop emailing files back and forth and start using version control properly. By the end of the day you will be able to track changes in your own files, collaborate with others through GitHub, and contribute to shared projects via pull requests.
No prior experience with Git or GitHub is required.

title: Introduction to Git & GitHub
text:
Learning Objectives

Module 1 — Concepts & GitHub

Explain what version control is and why it matters for research
Identify the difference between Git (the tool) and GitHub (the hosting platform)
Use key GitHub terminology: repository, commit, branch, fork, pull request
Create a GitHub account and first repository
Write formatted text using Markdown



Module 2 — Local Git

Initialize a Git repository on your machine
Describe the three-stage model: working directory → staging area → repository
Execute the core workflow: git add, git commit, git log
Inspect changes using git diff and understand HEAD



Module 3 — Branching & History

Create and switch between branches
Merge branches and resolve merge conflicts
Navigate commit history with git log and git checkout
Exclude files from tracking using .gitignore
Undo changes safely with git reset



Module 4 — GitHub Collaboration

Connect a local repository to GitHub using SSH
Share work with git push and receive updates with git pull
Clone an existing repository
Track tasks using GitHub Issues and Projects
Contribute to a shared repository via Fork and Pull Request

title: Introduction to Git & GitHub
text:
Learning Experiences
To reach these objectives we combine short concept explanations, live demos, and hands-on exercises. During exercises you are welcome to discuss with other participants. All exercise steps include expected commands and output so you can check your own progress.

title: Introduction to Git & GitHub
text:
License
This project is licensed under the MIT License.

title: Introduction to Git & GitHub
text:
Copyright
Copyright 2026, Deepak Tanwar

title: Git Cheat Sheet
text: A quick reference for all Git commands covered in the course.

title: Git Cheat Sheet
text:
Setup
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "email@example.com"
git config --list

title: Git Cheat Sheet
text:
Create a Repository
git init                 # new local repo in current directory
git clone <url>          # copy a remote repo to your machine

title: Git Cheat Sheet
text:
The Core Workflow
[working directory] --git add--> [staging area] --git commit--> [repository]
git status               # what has changed?
git add <file>           # stage a specific file
git add --all            # stage all changes
git commit -m "message"  # save staged changes with a message
git log                  # show full commit history
git log --oneline        # compact one-line history

title: Git Cheat Sheet
text:
Inspecting Changes
git diff                      # working directory vs staging area
git diff --staged             # staging area vs last commit
git diff HEAD                 # working directory vs last commit
git diff <hash1> <hash2>      # compare two commits

title: Git Cheat Sheet
text:
Branching
git branch                    # list branches
git branch <name>             # create a new branch
git checkout <name>           # switch to a branch
git checkout -b <name>        # create and switch in one step
git merge <branch>            # merge branch into current branch
git branch -d <name>          # delete a merged branch

title: Git Cheat Sheet
text:
Undoing Things
git reset <file>              # unstage a file
git reset --soft HEAD~1       # undo last commit, keep changes staged
git reset --hard HEAD~1       # undo last commit, discard all changes
git checkout -- <file>        # discard changes in working directory

title: Git Cheat Sheet
text:
History & Navigation
git log --oneline             # compact history with hashes
git checkout <hash>           # view a specific commit (detached HEAD)
git checkout main             # return to the latest main branch

title: Git Cheat Sheet
text:
Connecting to GitHub
git remote add origin <url>   # link local repo to GitHub
git remote -v                 # show remote connections
git push -u origin main       # push and set upstream (first time)
git push                      # push commits to GitHub
git pull                      # fetch + merge from GitHub
git fetch                     # download without merging

title: Git Cheat Sheet
text:
Collaboration Workflow
# Fork on GitHub, then:
git clone <your-fork-url>
git checkout -b my-feature
# make changes...
git add --all
git commit -m "describe what changed"
git push origin my-feature
# Open a Pull Request on GitHub

title: Git Cheat Sheet
text:
.gitignore
Create a .gitignore file in your repo root:
*.log           # ignore all .log files
data/raw/       # ignore a whole folder
secrets.txt     # ignore a specific file

title: Git Cheat Sheet
text:
Quick Reference Table



Command
What it does




git status
Always run this first — shows what changed


git add --all
Stage everything


git commit -m "msg"
Save a snapshot


git log --oneline
Quick history overview


git diff
What did I change since last stage?


git push
Send commits to GitHub


git pull
Get latest from GitHub


git checkout -b name
New branch + switch


git merge branch
Merge into current branch


git stash
Temporarily save uncommitted work


git stash pop
Restore stashed work

title: Pre-course Setup
text: Complete these steps before the course day. It will take about 20–30 minutes.

title: Pre-course Setup
text:
1. Create a GitHub Account
Go to https://github.com and sign up for a free account. Choose a professional username — you will share this publicly (e.g., on your CV).

title: Pre-course Setup
text:
2. Install Git

macOSWindowsLinux (Ubuntu/Debian)


Git is usually already installed. Open Terminal and run:
git --version
If not installed, you will be prompted to install Xcode Command Line Tools. Accept and wait.


Download and install Git for Windows. Use all default options. This also installs Git Bash — use that as your terminal during the course.


sudo apt update && sudo apt install git

title: Pre-course Setup
text:
3. Configure Git
Open Terminal (macOS/Linux) or Git Bash (Windows):
git config --global user.name "Your Name"
git config --global user.email "your.email@uzh.ch"
git config --global core.editor "nano"
Use the same email as your GitHub account. Verify:
git config --list

title: Pre-course Setup
text:
4. Set Up an SSH Key
SSH lets your computer talk to GitHub without typing a password every time.

Step 1 — Check for an existing key
ls -al ~/.ssh
If you see id_ed25519.pub or id_rsa.pub, skip to Step 3.


Step 2 — Generate a new key
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your.email@uzh.ch"
Press Enter to accept all defaults (no passphrase needed for the course).


Step 3 — Copy the public key
cat ~/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub
Select and copy the entire output (starts with ssh-ed25519).


Step 4 — Add the key to GitHub

Go to github.com/settings/keys
Click New SSH key
Title: My Laptop
Paste the key → Add SSH key



Step 5 — Test the connection
ssh -T git@github.com
Expected output:
Hi YOUR_USERNAME! You've successfully authenticated, but GitHub does not provide shell access.

title: Pre-course Setup
text:
5. Verify Everything Works
Run these three checks — all should succeed before the course day:
git --version        # git version 2.x.x or higher
git config user.name # your name
ssh -T git@github.com # Hi YOUR_USERNAME! ...






Note



If any step fails, email deepak.tanwar@uzh.ch with the error message and your operating system.

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
Introductions

Name
Designation & research group
Institution
Experience with Git and/or GitHub

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
A true story

“I had three weeks of analysis in one script. I ‘cleaned it up’ on Friday afternoon, deleted what looked like dead code, and saved over the file. Monday morning: the results don’t reproduce. There was no Friday-morning version anywhere. I had to redo it.”


Show of hands: who has lost work, or a working version of something, this way?


By the end of today, this will never happen to you again.

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
Why are we here?

“FINAL.doc” — a story everyone knows

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
The version control problem
A general example from manuscript revision:





flowchart LR
    A[Draft by student] --> B[Revised by PI]
    B --> C[Revised by student]
    C --> D[Finalized by student]






How would you name these files?


manuscript_FA.docx
manuscript_FA_LA.docx
manuscript_FA_LA_FA.docx
manuscript_final.docx

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
Or by date?

manuscript_20221001.docx
manuscript_20221224.docx
manuscript_final.docx








Warning


Both approaches break down: which file had the figure you liked? Which comment thread belongs to which version? Renaming files is not version control.

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
What is Git?
This is exactly the problem Git was built to solve.

A distributed version control system (VCS)
Tracks changes to files over time, with a message explaining why
Works locally — no internet needed
Free and open source

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
Git ≠ GitHub
These are often confused, but they’re different tools:

Git = the version control tool running on your machine
GitHub = a website that hosts Git repositories online

We’ll use both today — Git is what you use, GitHub is where you share.

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
Version control — the better way
One file. Many snapshots, each with a message explaining why it changed.





flowchart LR
    v1["v1: first draft"] --> v2["v2: revision 1"]
    v2 --> v3["v3: updated figures"]
    v3 --> v4["v4: bioRxiv submission"]
    v4 --> v5["v5: published"]






The filename never changes. The history does.

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
“Why not just use Google Docs?”
A fair question — for a single shared document, Google Docs works fine.

Git earns its complexity when you have:

Code and scripts, not prose — line-by-line diffs that mean something
Parallel experiments — try a new analysis approach without breaking the working one (branches)
Exact reproducibility — re-run the exact code that produced Figure 2, six months later

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
Why Git shines
The final advantage:

No internet required — full history available offline, on your machine

Every other tool requires a server connection. Git works everywhere — on planes, in the field, during internet outages.

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
section: Git vs Google Docs: Rule of thumb
text:
Git vs Google Docs: Rule of thumb






Note


Collaborating on prose? → Google Docs (faster, simpler)
Collaborating on code/analysis? → Git (reproducible, historical, safe experiments)

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
Types of Version Control
Git is one kind of version control system — here’s how it compares to the alternatives.
Local VCS

No collaboration possible — if your laptop dies, so does the history.

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
Git is winning

Git overtook SVN because every clone is a full backup — the distributed model won.

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
Why Git won
With Git’s distributed model:

Every copy is a full backup — no single point of failure
Everyone can work offline
Git is fast (no server communication needed)
Easy to merge work from many contributors

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
GitHub: Building on Git
GitHub built a free hosting platform on top of Git, adding:

Project management (Issues, Projects)
Automated testing (Actions)
Easy collaboration across teams
A social network for developers

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
The 6 core concepts — part 1
Git has hundreds of commands. You only need 6 ideas to be effective:

Repository — a folder Git is watching
Working directory — your files, right now, as you edit them
Staging area — a holding pen for what goes into the next snapshot

These are the “places” where your code lives during development.

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
The 6 core concepts — part 2
The rest of the 6:

Commit — a saved snapshot with a message
Branch — an independent line of commits
Remote — a copy of the repository living somewhere else (GitHub)

These are the “actions” you take with those places.

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
Your navigation tool






Tip


Everything from here on is one of these 6 things, or a relationship between two of them. If you ever feel lost, ask: “which of the 6 actions am I touching right now?”

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
GitHub Terminologies — Core
Now that you have the 6 concepts, here’s the vocabulary GitHub adds:



Term
What it means




Repository
A folder tracked by Git


Commit
A saved snapshot with a message


Branch
A separate line of development

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
GitHub Terminologies — Collaboration
Tools for working with others:







Term
What it means




Fork
Your own server-side copy of someone else’s repo


Pull Request
A proposal to merge your changes into the main repo


Issue
A task, bug report, or discussion thread


Organization
A shared account for teams

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
Applications in bioinformatics
With the concepts and vocabulary in place, here’s what they actually buy you:

Version control of analysis scripts and pipelines
Full history of every change — reproducible research
Collaboration across institutes and time zones
Issues and Projects for experiment planning
Genomic Data Science: version-controlled, reproducible data analysis

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
Writing on GitHub: Markdown
Before we open GitHub for real, one last tool: GitHub renders .md files as formatted text automatically. Structure first:
# Heading 1
## Heading 2

**bold**   *italic*   `inline code`

- list item 1
- list item 2

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
Markdown: tables & links
| Column 1 | Column 2 |
|----------|----------|
| row 1 a  | row 1 b  |

[link text](https://github.com)
You’ll use exactly this much Markdown in the next two exercises — nothing more.

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
Exercise 1

Create a GitHub account at github.com
Choose a professional username
Check if you belong to the DQBM-CCCZ organization
Create a new public repository named after your username
Go to https://github.com/YOUR_USERNAME
Take a screenshot

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
Exercise 2

Open the README.md file in your repository
Write your introduction using Markdown:

Name as # Heading 1
Sections: About Me, Research Project, Affiliation, Education
Education as a table with university and year columns

Commit with message: added introduction and education table
Compare your profile to the screenshot from Exercise 1

title: Module 1: Concepts & GitHub
text:
Ready for Module 2?
You now understand why Git exists and the core vocabulary.
Next: you’ll actually use Git on your machine. We’ll build a realistic project step-by-step: init → add → commit → log → diff.
Come back ready to type some commands. ⌨️

title: Module 3: Branching & History
text:
Module 3: Branches & parallel work
You’ve mastered the basic workflow: init → add → commit → log.
Now the real power: try new ideas without breaking what works. That’s what branches do.

title: Module 3: Branching & History
text:
Why branches?
Branches let you work on something new without breaking what already works.





gitGraph
   commit id: "QC done"
   commit id: "log2 normalization"
   branch try-new-normalization
   commit id: "quantile attempt"
   commit id: "compare results"
   checkout main
   commit id: "wrote methods section"
   merge try-new-normalization

title: Module 3: Branching & History
text:
Why branches? (continued)
In research: use a branch to try a new analysis approach. If it works → merge. If not → delete without any harm to main.






Tip


Discussion: what’s something you’ve wanted to try in your own analysis, but were afraid to risk breaking what already works?

title: Module 3: Branching & History
text:
5 reasons to branch

Experiment — try a different method without risking what works
Feature — build something new in isolation (e.g. a new plotting function)
Bug fix — fix something urgent without mixing in unrelated work-in-progress
Collaboration — you and a labmate work on the same project without colliding
Review — let a colleague review your changes before they reach main

title: Module 3: Branching & History
text:
Why branches? (summary)
Different reasons, same mechanism: branches are cheap, disposable, and safe.
Use them liberally. Don’t be afraid to create one, experiment, and delete it if it doesn’t work out.

title: Module 3: Branching & History
text:
git branch + git checkout
Time to do what the diagram a moment ago showed, for real:
# See which branch you are on
git branch

# Create a new branch for a new normalization approach
git branch try-new-normalization

# Switch to it
git checkout try-new-normalization

# Create AND switch in one step
git checkout -b try-new-normalization

title: Module 3: Branching & History
text:
Making commits on a branch
You’re now on the new branch. Commits here behave exactly like before — they just don’t exist anywhere else yet:
# You are on branch "try-new-normalization"
nano analysis_log.md    # document the alternative approach

git add analysis_log.md
git commit -m "tried quantile normalization instead of log2"

# Go back to main — this note does not exist here
git checkout main
cat analysis_log.md
Branches are isolated. Changes on a branch don’t appear on main until you merge.

title: Module 3: Branching & History
text:
git merge
# On main branch, once you decide the new approach is good:
git merge try-new-normalization
Git replays the commits from the branch onto main. After merging, you can delete the branch:
git branch -d try-new-normalization
That merge went smoothly because nothing else on main had touched the same file. Merges aren’t always this clean.

title: Module 3: Branching & History
text:
Merge conflicts
Conflicts happen when two branches change the same line differently — e.g. you and a labmate both edit the conclusion in results_summary.md.
<<<<<<< HEAD
Treated samples show a 2-fold increase in expression.
=======
Treated samples show no significant change in expression.
>>>>>>> try-new-normalization

title: Module 3: Branching & History
text:
Resolving merge conflicts
When you git merge and Git can’t automatically resolve, it stops and marks the conflicts.
How to resolve:

Open the file with conflict markers
Choose which version to keep (or combine them)
Remove all <<<<<<<, =======, >>>>>>> markers
git add <file> → git commit

Done! The merge completes.

title: Module 3: Branching & History
text:
Exploring git history
# Full history
git log

# Compact — one line per commit
git log --oneline

# Go to a specific commit (read-only — "detached HEAD")
git checkout a499ea4

# Return to the latest
git checkout main

title: Module 3: Branching & History
text:
Ignoring files with .gitignore
Create a file called .gitignore in your repo root:
# Ignore raw instrument output (too large/sensitive for GitHub)
raw_data/

# Ignore temporary analysis files
*.tmp

# Ignore a specific file
secrets.txt
git add .gitignore
git commit -m "added gitignore"
Git will never ask you about these files again.

title: Module 3: Branching & History
text:
git reset — undoing things







Command
Effect




git reset <file>
Unstage a file (keep changes)


git reset --soft HEAD~1
Undo last commit, keep changes staged


git reset --hard HEAD~1
Undo last commit, discard all changes










Warning


--hard is permanent. Use with care.

title: Module 3: Branching & History
text:
Exercise 4 — Branching & Merge Conflicts
See Exercise 4 for the full walkthrough.
Summary:

git checkout -b try-new-normalization
Edit analysis_log.md and results_summary.md, add, commit
git checkout main → git merge try-new-normalization
Create a conflict by editing the same conclusion on two branches
Resolve the conflict manually
Create raw_data/, add .gitignore, verify files are ignored

title: Module 3: Branching & History
text:
Ready for Module 4?
You’ve mastered local workflows and branches. Everything so far is on your machine.
Next: move to GitHub. Share your work online, collaborate with others, and learn the professional Pull Request workflow.
Your code is about to meet the world. 🌐
